Key Recommendations

✓
Stay the course. Protocol is working — fat coming off centrally, muscle holding peripherally.
↺
Yesterday (Sat 4/25) P3|C round 2 — 6 PRs. Bench 175 / Leg Press 388 / Cable Row 126 / DB Skull Crusher 45.5 / Hammer Curl 47.7 / Russian Twist 19.6 (all NEW PRs). 23 working sets, Challenging RPE, +20 min stairclimber. Three lifts tied at e1RM ceiling: Shoulder Press 105, Lat Pulldown 140, Hamstring Curl 140.
★
Today (Sun 4/26 — Wk 8 close): rest day + body measurements + body pics. Waist 32.91" (-0.47" vs Wk 7) — second-largest single-week waist drop of the cut. Shoulders 50.07" (+0.11" vs Wk 7) — new high. Bicep R 15.86" (+0.04"). Thigh R 24.99" (+0.31"). Chest 40.59" (flat — third consecutive week). W/H 0.826 (new low, below Wk 5 prior 0.835). S/W 1.521 (new high, above Wk 5 prior 1.519). Visual check (front/side/back): V-taper widening clearly visible — broad shoulders narrow to flatter waist; lat spread shows in back shot, biceps and forearms vascularized; modest abdominal definition with remaining lower-belly softness consistent with the 20.0% RENPHO read. Wk 8 finishes with avg weight 175.5 lbs, avg sleep 8.5 hrs, nutrition 100% (6/6 logged), 6-day step avg 19,553. Wk 9 starts tomorrow.
✓
Sleep solid; deep sleep watch continues. Wk 8 closes at 8.5 hrs avg across 7 nights (scores 75–94) — slightly above Wk 7's 8.5 hr avg. Last night (4/25→26) was the longest night of the week: 10h 5m / score 85 (Optimal) — REM 2h 35m (Optimal), light 6h 30m (Optimal), but deep dropped to 1h 0m (Needs Attention), the third Wk 8 night below the 1h 30m optimal threshold. Wk 8 deep-sleep distribution: only one Optimal night (4/23→24, 1h 40m); the other six all under 1h 30m (1h 0m, 1h 25m, 1h 15m, 1h 10m, 56m, 1h 0m). Total sleep volume good, but deep-sleep quality is the running theme — possibly tied to high training intensity overlapping with macro deficit.
!
Watch Saturday steps. Saturday activity averages just 9,865 steps (vs. 16,000+ on weekdays), with a low of 2,483 on Mar 7. A weekend morning walk could close the gap.
↑
Bump fat intake to 60–65g by trading some carbs. Hormonal support as BF% continues to drop.
!
Nutrition tracking 7/7 — Wk 8 closes at 100%. All seven days of Wk 8 logged Y. Protein margin progression across the week: Apr 22 P 155.2g (+0.2g — thinnest Y of the cut); Apr 23 157g (+2g); Apr 24 162.4g (+7.4g); Apr 25 164.2g (+9.2g); Apr 26 156.7g (+1.7g — second-thinnest of the week). Rest-day fat ceiling: Apr 20 (~68g) and Apr 22 (70.7g) ran over 65g, but Apr 23 (56.2g), Apr 24 (58.5g), Apr 25 (60.5g), and Apr 26 (56.6g) all landed inside the 54–65g window — four consecutive rest days in range. The early-week fat overshoot pattern has resolved. Rest-day carb overage persists: Apr 25 carbs 117.9g (+20.9g) and Apr 26 carbs 117.8g (+20.8g), the fourth and fifth consecutive rest days above the 97g target. Worth pulling 15–20g of carbs out of rest-day meals to land closer to spec.
★
Posture visibly improved. Side-view photos show a noticeably more upright stance compared to Apr 2025 — less anterior pelvic tilt, more engaged upper back. Heavy squats (e1RM 301) and deadlifts (e1RM 273) are paying off beyond the mirror.

Nutrition Compliance

Recovery Signal Guide

SignalMeaningAction
++ Progressinge1RM equal to or higher than previous sessionKeep going
!! Watche1RM dropped 1–5% from previous sessionCould be a bad day — monitor next session
XX Recovery Alerte1RM dropped >5% from previous sessionConsider a deload
DELOAD TRIGGER3+ lifts yellow/red same week, or any lift red 2+ sessionsTake a deload week

How the Tracking Works

The spreadsheet has two tabs: Workout Log for raw set-by-set entry (date, exercise, weight, reps, RPE) and Lift Progress which auto-calculates your best estimated 1RM per session using the Epley formula (weight × (1 + reps/30)), total session volume, and flags recovery signals based on session-over-session e1RM changes.

The big 5 tracked: Squat, Bench Press (flat → incline in Phase 2), Deadlift, Overhead Press, Barbell Row (rear delt → bent over in Phase 2). Phase 2 started Mar 17 with higher rep ranges (12s vs 8s) and exercise swaps. The e1RM trend lines carry over so you can see the full arc.

What to watch for: Declining e1RM across 2+ consecutive sessions for the same lift is the clearest signal of accumulated fatigue. At your caloric deficit, you have less recovery headroom than someone at maintenance — so catching early warning signs matters more. RPE trending upward for the same weight/reps is another subtle signal that fatigue is accumulating before performance actually drops.

Data loaded: 25 sessions (Feb 24 – Apr 25), 581 total sets across 38 exercises. Phase 1 (P1): 10 sessions; Phase 2 (P2): 9 sessions; Phase 3 (P3): 6 sessions.

InBody Cross-Reference (Apple Health)

116 InBody BIA readings from Jan 2023 – Oct 2025 were extracted from Apple Health. During the journey period (Apr–Oct 2025), InBody showed a consistent downward trend from ~24.5% to ~17.6% — tracking the same direction as DEXA but reading 5–8 percentage points lower. This offset is typical for BIA devices, which underestimate body fat relative to DEXA. The InBody trend is valuable confirmation that body fat was declining steadily through October 2025, even though the absolute numbers shouldn't be taken at face value.

Purple triangles on the chart above show InBody readings aligned to the nearest journey date. No InBody data after Oct 23, 2025. RENPHO bioimpedance scale added Mar 2026: reads 20.1–21.1% BF, consistent with DEXA #5 (22.3%) after typical bioimpedance underestimate. *Feb 26 DEXA was creatine/glycogen-depleted; raw 23.7% overstated by ~1.4pp (confirmed by DEXA #5).

What the Photos Show

1
Apr → Sep 2025 (5 months): Face notably leaner, love handles reduced, midsection slimmed. ~21 lbs lost while maintaining muscle mass underneath.
2
Jan 2026 (post-holiday): Visible regression — face slightly fuller, midsection softer. ~1 lb regained but the BF% estimate ticked up to 26%, suggesting fat gain with some muscle loss.
3
Feb → Mar 2026 (structured cut): The most dramatic visual change. Midsection significantly flatter, hint of upper ab definition, shoulders/arms noticeably more defined. ~25 lbs down from starting photos.

Spice Build #1 — Smoky

Onion Powder, 2.5g (1 tsp)90.3g0g2.0g0.2g
Chili Powder, 2.5g (1 tsp)70.3g0.4g1.2g0.9g

Salt, black pepper listed in recipe but zero-calorie — not included in totals.

Recipe

1. Base

Add 2 lbs boneless skinless chicken breast to the slow cooker.
Add ½ cup low-sodium chicken broth, 2 tbsp apple cider vinegar, 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp black pepper, and 4 smashed garlic cloves.

2. Spice Build #1 — Smoky (Adjusted)

Add 1.5 tbsp smoked paprika, 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp onion powder, 1 tsp chili powder, and a pinch of cayenne directly to the slow cooker.
Add 1 tbsp Worcestershire sauce and 2 tbsp tomato paste.
Stir or turn the chicken to coat evenly in the spices and liquids.

3. Cook & Shred

Cook on LOW for 6–7 hours or HIGH for 3–4 hours.
Shred the chicken directly in the slow cooker using two forks.
Let the shredded chicken sit for 10 minutes to reabsorb the cooking liquid before serving.
Tip for accurate tracking: Weigh the finished batch after shredding. Divide the whole-recipe totals (1,216 cal / 209.6g P / 26.5g F / 24.3g C) by the actual cooked weight in hundreds to get your true per-100g values. Slow cooker moisture retention varies — actual yield typically falls between 750–850g.

Base

Part-Skim Mozzarella, 100g30225.0g20.0g3.5g0g
Parmesan, 33g13911.8g9.4g1.1g0g
Sofrito
Onion, 250–300g1163.2g0.3g27.0g4.9g
Carrots, 250–300g1192.6g0.6g27.8g8.1g
Celery, 250–300g412.0g0.6g8.7g4.6g
Garlic, 3 cloves (~9g)130.6g0.0g3.0g0.2g
Sauce & Flavor
EVOO, ~16g retained1410g16.0g0g0g
Tomato Paste, 20g160.9g0.1g3.8g0.8g
Mutti Crushed Tomatoes, 732g1909.5g0.7g37.3g8.8g
Beef Bouillon, 20g343.4g1.6g1.4g0g
Dry Gelatin, 14g4712.0g0.0g0g0g
Water, 512g00g0g0g0g
TOTAL4,031309.1g115.1g440.4g41.1g

Veggie weights are flexible (250–300g each) — per-100g macros shift <0.1g across that range.

Recipe

1. Pasta Preparation

Bring a large pot of salted water to a boil.
Add the 454g of penne and cook for 11 minutes until al dente.
Drain and set aside.

2. Sauté the Sofrito

Heat 14g of olive oil in a large pan over medium heat.
Add the 290g each of onions, carrots, and celery.
Cook slowly for approximately 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the vegetables are soft and caramelized.
Remove the vegetables from the pan and set them aside.

3. The Lean Meat Sear

Add another 14g of olive oil to the pan and increase heat to medium-high.
Add the 2 lbs of ground beef. Let it sit untouched for 1 minute to brown, then flip and break it up with a spatula.
Once the beef is mostly browned, add the final 3g of olive oil to a small cleared spot in the pan and sauté the minced garlic for 30 seconds.
The Lean Trick: Before adding any other ingredients, use a spoon or tilt the pan to pour off and remove all excess grease and oil from the beef and garlic mixture.
Draining removes ~35% of the rendered fat, cutting beef fat from 10g to ~6.5g per 100g.

4. Building the Sauce

Add the 20g of tomato paste to the beef and stir for 1 minute.
Add the sautéed vegetables back into the pan.
Pour in the 732g of crushed tomatoes, 512g of water, 20g of bouillon, and 14g of gelatin.
Simmering: Let the sauce simmer uncovered. Because you used crushed tomatoes, reduce the sauce until it is thick and "jammy" — it should leave a clear trail on the bottom of the pan when you move a spoon through it.
Season with salt, oregano, and crushed red pepper to taste.

5. Assembly and Broil

Preheat your oven's broiler.
In a large baking dish, combine the cooked pasta and the thickened meat sauce.
Evenly top with the 100g of mozzarella and 33g of grated Parmesan.
Place under the broiler for about 2 minutes until the cheese is bubbly and golden and some pasta tips are slightly crispy.
Tip for accurate tracking: Weigh the finished casserole, then divide the whole-recipe totals (4,031 cal / 309g P / 115g F / 440g C / 41g fiber) by the weight in hundreds to get your true per-100g values.

Baby spinach, 60g141.7g0.2g2.2g1.3g
Kimchi, 45g70.5g0.2g1.1g0.7g
Oats
Bob's Red Mill Steel Cut Oats, 40g dry1525.3g2.6g27.1g4.2g
TOTAL48047.4g13.1g41.1g8.5g

Seasonings (salt, pepper, garlic powder) not listed — effectively zero calories.

Recipe

1. Steel Cut Oats

Bring water to a boil in a small saucepan. Add 40g Bob's Red Mill steel cut oats and a pinch of salt.
Reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 20–25 minutes, stirring occasionally, until oats are tender and water is absorbed.

2. Egg Scramble

Crack 2 whole eggs into a bowl and add 240g liquid egg whites. Season with salt, pepper, and garlic powder. Whisk to combine.
Dice 75g red bell pepper and 40g red onion (small pieces — about 1/4″ cubes). Heat a nonstick skillet over medium heat.
Add the diced onion and bell pepper to the dry skillet. Sauté for 5–7 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the onion is translucent and the peppers are tender-crisp. If the pan looks dry, add a small splash of water (1–2 tsp) rather than oil.
Add 60g baby spinach to the skillet. Toss for ~1 minute until just wilted — the spinach will collapse to roughly 1/4 its raw volume.
Pour in the egg mixture. Let it set for about 30 seconds, then gently fold and scramble until eggs are just cooked through — don't overcook.

3. Plate

Transfer the scramble to a plate. Serve the steel cut oats alongside. Top or side with 45g kimchi.
Timing note: Start the oats first — they take 20–25 minutes. Begin the scramble when the oats have about 8–10 minutes left (the raw bell pepper and onion need a longer sauté). Everything finishes at the same time.

Ingredient-by-Ingredient Breakdown

IngredientCalProFatCarbsFiber
Vegetables
Baby/fingerling potatoes, 311g (~11 oz)2396.2g0.3g54.4g6.5g
Carrots, 183g (3 medium, ½" diagonals)751.6g0.4g17.6g5.1g
Broccoli florets, 283g (~1 small head)967.9g1.1g18.7g7.4g
Fat & Aromatics
Olive oil, 28g (2 tbsp)2480.0g28.0g0.0g0.0g
Garlic, 3 cloves (~9g), minced130.6g0.0g3.0g0.2g
Lemon juice, ~15g (½ lemon) + zest30.1g0.0g1.0g0.0g
Seasonings (not counted — zero-cal)
Smoked paprika, rosemary, thyme, kosher salt, black pepper—————
TOTAL (whole tray)67416.4g29.8g94.7g19.2g

Veggie weights are flexible ±10% — per-100g macros shift <5 cal across that range. Olive oil stays in the dish (no draining), so the full 28g counts.

Recipe

1. Prep & Seasoning Blend

Preheat oven to 425°F. Line a sheet pan with parchment.
Halve baby/fingerling potatoes into ~1″ pieces (or cube Yukon Golds to the same size).
Cut carrots into ½″ diagonals (or halve baby carrots lengthwise).
Break broccoli into uniform florets.
In a small bowl combine: 3 cloves minced garlic, 1 tsp smoked paprika, ½ tsp dried rosemary (or 1 tsp fresh), ½ tsp dried thyme, ¾ tsp kosher salt, and black pepper to taste. This is your seasoning blend.

2a. Method A — Sheet-Pan Basic (~35 min total)

Toss potatoes + carrots with 1½ tbsp of the olive oil and half the seasoning blend. Spread in a single layer on the sheet pan. Roast 15 minutes.
Toss broccoli with the remaining ½ tbsp olive oil and remaining seasoning blend.
Pull the pan, push the potatoes and carrots to one side, and add the broccoli in a single layer. Roast another 15–18 minutes, tossing once, until the potatoes are fork-tender and the broccoli tips are crispy-brown.
Off the heat, hit everything with the zest of ½ a lemon and a squeeze of juice.

2b. Method B — Smashed-Potato Variant (~45 min total)

Parboil whole small potatoes for 10 minutes, until just fork-tender. Drain and pat dry.
On the sheet pan, press each potato with the bottom of a glass until it cracks open and flattens to about ½″ thick. Drizzle with 1 tbsp olive oil and half the seasoning blend. Roast at 425°F for 15 minutes.
Add seasoned carrots (tossed with 1 tsp of the remaining oil) to the tray. Roast 10 more minutes.
Add seasoned broccoli (tossed with the last 1 tsp oil and remaining seasoning) to the tray. Roast a final 12–15 minutes until the smashed potatoes are crispy-edged and the broccoli tips are browning.
Lemon zest + squeeze finish as in Method A.

3. Pairing & Protein Notes

Chicken (most common)
Season a 6–8 oz chicken breast (or thighs) with the same rub before cooking. Grill or pan-sear. The shared seasoning makes the plate feel like one dish instead of protein + side. Bump the lemon if using thighs.
Lean sirloin
Drop the rosemary, sub ½ tsp onion powder + pinch of cayenne. The smokier, slightly spicier profile cuts through beef richness better than the herb-forward chicken version.

4. Macro-Tuning Lever

To keep carbs under the 97g rest-day ceiling when pairing with chicken: drop the potato portion to ~6 oz (170g) and bump broccoli to ~14 oz (400g) to keep the plate full. That swap trims ~77 cal and drops whole-recipe carbs from 95g to ~70g (47g to ~35g at 2 servings).
Tip for accurate tracking: Log the recipe in your macro app at 96 cal / 2.3g P / 4.3g F / 13.5g C / 2.7g fiber per 100g cooked. Weigh the finished sheet-pan yield on your first cook and divide the whole-recipe totals (674 / 16.4 / 29.8 / 94.7 / 19.2) by the actual gram weight ÷ 100 to calibrate — estimated here at ~700g, but your real yield may shift ±50g depending on oven temp and roast time. Then weigh your plate to the gram every time and multiply by the per-100g values.

Recipe Instructions

1. Sauce

Combine 18 oz tomato paste, 64 oz tomato juice, 1 cup grated parmesan cheese, 3 bay leaves, 1½ tsp red pepper seeds, 2 whole cloves garlic, and ½ tsp salt in a heavy pot.
Just put all together.
Cook on medium until boiling, then lower to low.

2. Meatballs

Combine 2 pounds 90/10 ground beef, ½ cup grated Italian cheese (mozzarella — shredded is fine), 1 cup bread crumbs, 1 clove garlic (cut fine), 1 tsp salt, and 3 eggs.

TOTAL (1 fillet)35437.4g20.8g1.7g0.15g

Costco Kirkland Wild Sockeye comes in 6 oz (170g) individually vacuum-packed portions. Cooked weight is ~140g (cooking loss ~18%). Per 100g cooked: 253 cal / 26.7g P / 14.9g F.

Recipe

1. Thaw (or skip if cooking from frozen)

Pull a vacuum-sealed portion from the freezer the night before and place in the fridge.
Quick-thaw alternative: submerge sealed packet in cold water for 25–30 min. Do not microwave.
If cooking from frozen, skip this step entirely — see Step 4 alternate timing.

2. Preheat & Prep

Preheat oven to 400°F (or 425°F if cooking from frozen).
Line a sheet pan with parchment paper.
Place fillet skin-side down on the parchment. The skin protects the flesh and crisps slightly during cooking.

3. Season

Drizzle 1/2 tablespoon (7g) of olive oil over the fillet.
Season with kosher salt, freshly cracked black pepper, and a pinch of garlic powder if using. Smoked paprika optional for color.
Squeeze juice from half a lemon over the top. Save the zest for finishing if you have a microplane.

4. Roast

Thawed: 12–15 minutes at 400°F. From frozen: 18–22 minutes at 425°F.
The fillet is done when a fork just barely flakes the thickest part. Internal temp: 125–130°F (medium, slightly translucent center) is ideal — do not exceed 135°F.
Most home cooks overcook salmon. Pulling at 125°F gives you the silky texture restaurants get. The carryover heat takes it to 130°F off the pan.

5. Finish & Serve

Squeeze fresh lemon over the cooked fillet. Add lemon zest and chopped fresh dill if available.
Plate with 200g of the Garlic-Paprika Roasted Trio for a complete dinner. Both can roast on the same sheet pan if you start the trio 25 min before the salmon goes in.
Same-pan dinner protocol: Start the Garlic-Paprika Trio at 400°F. After 25 min, pull the pan, push the veggies to one side, add the salmon (parchment-protected), and return to the oven for 12–15 min more. Total active time: ~5 min. Total elapsed: 40 min. One pan, no cleanup overlap.
Tracking tip: If you log this in your macros app, use 100g cooked = 253 cal / 26.7g P / 14.9g F / 0g C. Or just log "salmon fillet" at 354 cal / 37g P / 21g F per fillet.

Eat one serving for lunch, save the second portion in the fridge for tomorrow's lunch (sealed container, 2–3 days). On a rest day with carbs at 6.2g, leaves 90g of headroom to spend on dinner carbs.

Ingredient-by-Ingredient Breakdown (whole recipe)

IngredientCalProFatCarbsFiber
Protein
Wild Planet wild sockeye salmon, 2 cans (254g drained)41973.7g11.9g0g0g
Plain nonfat Greek yogurt (Fage 0%), 200g11820.6g0.8g7.2g0g
Flavor & Tang
Dijon mustard, 15g (~1 tbsp)90.7g0.5g1.0g0.1g
Lemon juice, 15g (~1 tbsp)30g0g1.0g0.05g
Capers, 10g (~1 tsp drained)20.2g0.1g0.5g0.3g
Aromatics
Red onion, 25g (~2 tbsp finely diced)100.3g0g2.3g0.4g
Fresh dill, 6g (~2 tsp chopped)30.2g0.1g0.4g0.1g
Seasonings (not counted — zero-cal)
Black pepper, garlic powder (optional)—————
TOTAL (whole recipe, ~525g)56495.7g13.4g12.4g0.95g

Per 100g of finished salad: 107 cal / 18.2g P / 2.6g F / 2.4g C. Salmon brand can substitute (Wild Planet, Trader Joe's, Costco Kirkland canned wild). Avoid pink salmon — sockeye has higher omega-3 density.

Recipe

1. Drain & Flake the Salmon

Open both cans of Wild Planet wild sockeye salmon. Drain the liquid completely — press lid against fish to squeeze out excess water/oil.
Empty drained salmon into a medium mixing bowl. Use a fork to flake into bite-sized pieces. Don't over-shred — you want some structure to the chunks.
If your can has visible bones, mash them with the fork — they're soft, edible, and a meaningful calcium source. Most people don't notice them once mixed in.

2. Build the Dressing

In a small bowl, whisk together 200g of plain nonfat Greek yogurt, 15g (~1 tbsp) of Dijon mustard, and 15g (~1 tbsp) of fresh lemon juice.
Season with freshly cracked black pepper to taste. Add a pinch of garlic powder if you like a bit of bite.
Skip salt initially — the capers and Dijon are already salty. Taste before adjusting.

3. Combine & Fold

Finely dice 25g of red onion (~2 tbsp). Drain and roughly chop 10g of capers (~1 tsp). Finely chop 6g of fresh dill (~2 tsp loosely packed).
Add the dressing to the flaked salmon. Fold gently with a spatula until the salmon is evenly coated. Add red onion, capers, and chopped dill. Fold once more.
Taste and adjust: more lemon if it needs brightness, more pepper if it needs sharpness, more dill if you want it to lean Scandinavian.

4. Serve or Store

Eat immediately: Serve over a bed of mixed greens, scoop with cucumber slices, or spread on whole-grain toast.
Store for later: Transfer to a sealed container. Refrigerate 2–3 days. Flavors deepen overnight — the next-day version is arguably better than fresh.
If meal-prepping for the week, double the recipe. The whole batch is ~190g of protein for ~$8 of canned salmon.
Why this works for the cut: Per-serving cal-to-protein ratio is 5.9:1, which is on the low end of what's achievable with real food (Greek yogurt + lean canned fish are both extreme protein-density staples). Provides EPA + DHA from the salmon (filling the omega-3 gap from a no-fish week), calcium from the yogurt, no cooking required. Compare to a 282-cal portion of the chocolate milkshake + pistachios pairing (16g P) — this recipe gives you 3x the protein at the same calorie cost.
Tracking tip: If you log this in your macros app, use 100g = 107 cal / 18.2g P / 2.6g F / 2.4g C. A standard 263g serving (half the recipe) is 282 cal / 48g P / 6.7g F.

TOTAL (whole recipe, ~525g)601100.7g11.4g21.5g3.3g

Per 100g of finished salad: 114 cal / 19.2g P / 2.2g F / 4.1g C. Pulled chicken values pulled from your Slow Cooker Smoky Pulled Chicken recipe (152 cal / 26.25g P / 3.31g F per 100g cooked).

Recipe

1. Pull from the Batch

Pull 300g of pre-cooked smoky pulled chicken from the fridge (or thaw if frozen). The chicken should already be shredded from the slow-cooker batch.
If you don't have pre-cooked pulled chicken on hand, you can sub a 200g grilled chicken breast diced small — the macros shift slightly higher in protein and lower in fat, but the recipe works either way.
Empty into a medium mixing bowl. Break up any large clumps with a fork — you want bite-sized pieces evenly distributed.

2. Build the Dressing

In a small bowl, whisk together 200g of plain nonfat Greek yogurt, 15g (~1 tbsp) of Dijon mustard, and 15g (~1 tbsp) of fresh lemon juice.
Season with freshly cracked black pepper to taste. Add a pinch of smoked paprika to echo the BBQ rub on the chicken, or garlic powder if you want extra bite.
Skip salt initially — the pulled chicken's rub plus the capers will already deliver salt. Taste before adjusting.

3. Combine & Fold

Finely dice 25g of red onion (~2 tbsp). Drain and roughly chop 10g of capers (~1 tsp). Finely chop 6g of fresh dill (~2 tsp loosely packed).
Add the dressing to the pulled chicken. Fold gently with a spatula until the chicken is evenly coated. Add red onion, capers, and chopped dill. Fold once more.
Taste and adjust: more lemon if it needs brightness, more pepper if it needs sharpness, more dill if you want it to lean Scandinavian, more smoked paprika if you want the BBQ-rub character to come through.

4. Serve or Store

Eat immediately: Serve over a bed of mixed greens, scoop with cucumber slices, wrap in butter lettuce leaves, or spread on whole-grain toast.
Store for later: Transfer to a sealed container. Refrigerate 2–3 days. Flavors deepen overnight — the next-day version is arguably better than fresh.
Doubles cleanly. A 4-serving batch of this + 4 servings of the salmon salad = 8 lunches prepped on Sunday. Mix-and-match across the week for variety.
Why this works for the cut: Per-serving cal-to-protein ratio is 6.0:1, basically tied with the Greek Yogurt Salmon Salad as the lowest in your recipe library. Uses chicken from your existing slow-cooker batch (zero new prep effort) plus the same yogurt-based dressing template — one set of dressing ingredients, two completely different lunch options. Pair this and the salmon salad in rotation to get fish 3–4 days/week and chicken 2–3 days/week without your lunch slot ever feeling repetitive.
Tracking tip: If you log this in your macros app, use 100g = 114 cal / 19.2g P / 2.2g F / 4.1g C. A standard 263g serving (half the recipe) is 300 cal / 50.4g P / 5.7g F.
